title: Clone Wars multimedia project
content:
{{Top|rwm|imp|title=''Clone Wars'' multimedia project}}
{{Youmay|the 2002–2005 multimedia project|the [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars (multimedia project)|multimedia project based on the CGI animated series]] or [[Clone Wars (disambiguation)|other uses of the word]]}}
[[File:CW_Title.png|thumb|right|250px|The ''Clone Wars'' logo superimposed over a starfield for the [[Star Wars: Clone Wars|TV series]]]]

The '''''Clone Wars'' multimedia project''' was a three-year project created by [[Lucasfilm]], in conjunction with [[Del Rey]], [[Dark Horse Comics]], [[Cartoon Network]], and [[LucasArts]].

Set largely between the films [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|''Attack of the Clones'']] and [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Revenge of the Sith'']] (in terms of both in-universe and real-life chronology), the Clone Wars multimedia project attempted to tell the story of the [[Clone Wars/Legends|Clone Wars]] in real time<ref name="SWBoards">{{SWBoards|url=thread.jspa?threadID=212251&start=30#10644261|thread=Revenge of the Sith EU|user=[[Leland Chee|Tasty Taste]]|date=2005-05-06|time=8:45 AM}}</ref>—through [[List of books|books]], [[Star Wars comic books|comics]], [[Wikipedia:television|television]], and [[Star Wars video games|video game]]s. This echoes the previous project ''[[Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire]]''.

==Timeline {{SectionFlag}}==
The Multimedia Project followed an expanding time line during the three years between ''Attack of the Clones'' and ''Revenge of the Sith''{{'s}} releases. The time line itself could be found in the books marked "A Clone Wars Novel" and the [[Star Wars: Boba Fett (novel series)|Boba Fett]] series. Each medium was listed along with how many months after the [[First Battle of Geonosis/Legends|Battle of Geonosis]] it took place.

==Legacy==
[[File:Shatterpoint-Legends.png|[[Shatterpoint (novel)|''Shatterpoint'']] under the Legends banner|thumb|200px]]
On [[April 25]], [[2014]], the contents of the ''Clone Wars'' multimedia project were declared part of the continuity of ''Star Wars'' Legends and non-[[canon]] as a whole, though certain book titles would remain in print under the "Legends" banner.<ref name="Legends">{{SW|url=news/the-legendary-star-wars-expanded-universe-turns-a-new-page|text=The Legendary Star Wars Expanded Universe Turns a New Page}}</ref> Prior to this declaration, much of the material within the ''Clone Wars'' multimedia project had been directly contradicted by material from ''[[Star Wars: The Clone Wars]]'', which often told its stories without regard to continuity previously established within this project and was declared part of the new canon continuity. One such example was that the episode "[[Nightsisters (episode)|Nightsisters]]" presented an entirely different background for the character of [[Asajj Ventress/Legends|Asajj Ventress]] from that shown in previous material.<ref name="Nightsisters">{{TCW|Nightsisters (episode)}}</ref><ref name="Hate and Fear">[[Republic 60|''Republic'' 60]]</ref><ref name="CWC6">{{CW|6}}</ref><ref name="CWC7">{{CW|7}}</ref>

==Media==
===Television {{SectionFlag}}===
{{Main|Star Wars: Clone Wars}}

[[File:Grievouscartoon.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Introduction of General Grievous in the TV show]]
The television aspect of the Clone Wars project was covered by ''[[Star Wars: Clone Wars]]'', directed by [[Genndy Tartakovsky]]. This "micro-series" consisted at first of two seasons: ([[Clone Wars: Season One|Season One]], from [[November 7]] to [[November 20|20]] of [[2003]], and [[Clone Wars: Season Two|Season Two]], from [[March 26]] to [[April 8]] of [[2004]]), each consisting of ten episodes, each roughly three minutes in length. In addition to movie characters, the show used several characters from the ''[[Star Wars: Republic]]'' comics from [[Dark Horse Comics]], and was notable both for introducing the character of [[General/Legends|General]] [[Grievous/Legends|Grievous]] in [[Chapter 20 (Star Wars: Clone Wars)|Chapter 20]] and for promoting Anakin Skywalker from [[Padawan/Legends|Padawan]] to [[Jedi Knight/Legends|Jedi Knight]] in [[Chapter 21 (Star Wars: Clone Wars)|Chapter 21]].

Initially, the episodes were meant as a form of commercials for the other products of the Clone Wars multimedia project. But after the series became a huge success and the first two seasons won an [[Wikipedia:Emmy Award|Emmy Award]] for "Outstanding Animated Program," they were continued in [[Clone Wars: Season Three|Season Three]], consisting of five 12- to 15-minute episodes. These episodes detailed the timeframe leading up to [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Star Wars'': Episode III ''Revenge of the Sith'']]. Although criticized by fans (primarily for not being consistent with the Episode III tie-in novel ''[[Labyrinth of Evil]]'') they were also a hit, and led to [[George Lucas]] announcing plans to continue the show as a half-hour, 3D cartoon, which began in [[2008]] under the title [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars|''The Clone Wars'']].

The collected episodes of the show were made available on DVD in [[Star Wars: Clone Wars Volume One|''Star Wars: Clone Wars'' Volume One]] and [[Star Wars: Clone Wars Volume Two|Volume Two]]. The style of animation for the show was the inspiration for the [[Star Wars: Clone Wars Adventures (graphic novels)|''Clone Wars Adventures'']] comics. Many of the show's voice actors went on to reprise their character(s)' roles for various Clone Wars–related computer games, or had come from such roles to the show.

===Books and short stories {{SectionFlag}}===
[[File:CloneWarsNovel.png|center]]
Seven official novels were written in the Clone Wars series. They were each given the designation "A Clone Wars novel."
[[File:Shatterpoint_Cover.jpg|thumb|right|225px|''Shatterpoint'', the first Clone Wars novel]]

*[[Shatterpoint (novel)|''Shatterpoint'']]
*''[[Legacy of the Jedi]]'' (young-readers novel)
*''[[The Cestus Deception]]''
*''[[MedStar I: Battle Surgeons]]''
*''[[MedStar II: Jedi Healer]]''
*''[[Jedi Trial]]''
*''[[Yoda: Dark Rendezvous]]''

The [[Star Wars: Boba Fett (novel series)|''Boba Fett'']] young-adult book series also bears the Clone Wars brand. Another novel, ''[[Escape from Dagu]]'', was finished but was replaced with the ''Yoda: Dark Rendezvous''. All Clone Wars books were published by [[Del Rey]]. Another series of the Clone Wars multimedia project are the ''[[Republic Commando]]'' novels following the game of the same name. Although they were not introduced until two and half years into the project, only ''[[Republic Commando: Hard Contact]]'' and [[Star Wars: Republic Commando|the game]] are part of the Clone Wars Multimedia Project time line due to the others being released after ''Revenge of the Sith''.

''[[Star Wars Insider]]'' also published short stories for the series. They are [[The Pengalan Tradeoff|''The Clone Wars: The Pengalan Tradeoff'']], the three-part ''[[Hero of Cartao]]'', and [[The League of Spies|''League of Spies'']].

Although [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones (novelization)|''Star Wars Episode II: Attack of the Clones'']], ''[[Republic Commando: Triple Zero]]'', ''[[Republic Commando: True Colors]]'', ''[[Order 66: A Republic Commando Novel]]'', ''[[Labyrinth of Evil]]'', [[Star Wars: Revenge of the Sith (novelization)|''Star Wars Episode III: Revenge of the Sith'']], and ''[[Dark Lord: The Rise of Darth Vader]]'' are set in this era and cover the Clone Wars, they are not considered part of the Clone Wars book series.

Four short stories and e-books were also written as part of the Clone Wars series. These were ''[[The Hive]]'', ''[[Storm Fleet Warnings]]'', [[Equipment (short story)|''Equipment'']], and [[Duel (short story)|''Duel'']] (the last three of which were formed into the ''[[Star Wars: Short Story Collection| Short Story Collection]]'').

===Comics {{SectionFlag}}===
[[File:Swr49cover.jpg|thumb|left|150px|''Sacrifice'', the first Clone Wars comic]]

The comics segment of the Clone Wars storyline was told primarily through the ''[[Star Wars: Republic]]'' serial, starting with [[Republic 49|Issue 49]] (four issues after the comic had been renamed from ''Star Wars'' to ''Star Wars: Republic'') and continuing until the final [[Republic 83|Issue 83]]. It was in the ''Republic'' comics that [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Anakin Skywalker]] got the scar on his face that he has in Episode III.

The five-part [[Star Wars: Obsession|''Obsession'']] and [[Star Wars: Jedi (one-shots)|''Jedi'']] comics, and the [[Free Comic Book Day 2005 Special|''Star Wars—Free Comic Book Day 2005 Special'']], [[Purge (comic book)|''Purge'']], and ''[[Routine Valor]]'' one-shots, were tie-ins to the ''Republic'' Clone Wars storyline. Together, the comics were collected into the nine-part [[Star Wars: Clone Wars (trade paperbacks)|''Star Wars: Clone Wars'' trade-paperback]] series.

A collection of six [[Star Wars: Clone Wars Adventures (graphic novels)|''Clone Wars Adventures'']] comics (four more have been added since), based on the art of the TV show, were also released. Several of the stories from latter ''[[Star Wars Tales]]'' also tied in to the Clone Wars storyline, with varying levels of [[Star Wars Legends|continuity]]. From May to September 2002, the [[Star Wars: Jango Fett: Open Seasons|''Jango Fett: Open Seasons'']] comic (the counterpart to the [[Star Wars: Bounty Hunter|''Bounty Hunter'']] game) was released; while not part of the Clone wars series, it depicted the events that led to the creation of the [[Grand Army of the Republic/Legends|clone army]].

All Clone Wars comics were published by [[Dark Horse Comics]].

===Video games {{SectionFlag}}===
[[File:TCW-GameCube.jpg|thumb|right|Cover to ''The Clone Wars'']]

The majority of Clone Wars games were released in [[2002]]. Two of these were [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars (video game)|''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'']], released on [[November 28]], and ''[[Star Wars: Bounty Hunter]]'', a game that centered on the buildup to the [[Grand Army of the Republic/Legends|clone army's]] formation and a tie-in to the [[Star Wars: Jango Fett: Open Seasons|''Jango Fett: Open Seasons'']] comic. The game [[Star Wars: Republic Commando|''Republic Commando'']] was released on [[March 1]], 2005.

Other games depicting the Clone Wars are ''[[Star Wars: Battlefront]]'', which contained several levels depicting both original and movie, comic, or game-based Clone Wars battles, released on [[September 20]], [[2004]]. The sequel, ''[[Star Wars: Battlefront II]]'', again with Clone Wars missions, was released on [[November 1]], [[2005]]. ''[[Star Wars: Jedi Starfighter]]'' was released [[March 10]] in 2002, with ''[[Star Wars: Galactic Battlegrounds: Clone Campaigns]]'' released shortly after Episode II and the [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith (video game)|''Episode III'' video game]] ([[May 5]], 2005).

Many of the computer-game characters had the same voice actors as in the ''[[Star Wars: Clone Wars]]'' show.

===Toys {{SectionFlag}}===
[[Hasbro]] and other ''Star Wars'' licensees have produced many toys to tie in to both the multimedia project and the more recent TV series.
